# Ygg

A spatial code viewer for the age of AI-generated code.

## What this is

**Ygg is a code *reviewer*, not an editor.** In 2026, humans don't write most of the code anymore — agents do. The bottleneck has shifted from authoring to *understanding*: reading diffs generated by Claude Code, Codex, and others, verifying that the silicon got it right, and approving (or rejecting) the change.

Current code review tools are stuck in 1970s `diff`-style two-column red/green text walls. They were fine when humans wrote 15 lines at a time. They collapse when an agent produces a 40-file, 2000-line PR in 10 minutes. Ygg is built for that world.

The name Ygg is a shortening of Yggdrasil — the Norse world tree. Odin hung on it for nine days to obtain the knowledge of runes. A developer hangs on a PR for however long it takes to obtain the knowledge of what the agent wrote. The metaphor is intentional: Ygg visualizes code as trees at every level (file tree, class structure, AST, git history, branches) and exists to help humans read runes.

Short form: `ygg` (the CLI). Longer form: Yggdrasil (when we want gravitas).

## Guiding principles

### This is a viewer, not an editor
No cursor, no text input, no undo/redo, no autocomplete, no IME. Read-only. This massively simplifies the engineering surface — we don't need to compete with Zed or VS Code on text editing. We compete on *comprehension*.

### Beautiful is functional
The visual language is not decorative. Animation is *how the eye tracks change* — a moved method that slides visibly is understood without re-reading. A deleted block that flashes red and fades shrinks cognitive load vs. spotting red lines in a wall of text. FUI-inspired aesthetics (see Mark Coleran's work on Bourne, Children of Men, The Island) are a tool, not a style choice.

### Semantic over syntactic
Ygg renders *meaning*, not just tokens. A class attribute vs. a dataclass field share syntax but have different semantics — Ygg shows them differently. A `@classmethod` attached to the class "armature" vs. an instance method hanging from `self` — shown differently. Private vs. public — shown differently. This requires AST-level + semantic-rule awareness per language, not just tree-sitter coloring.

### Scrubbing, not stepping
Navigation through history is continuous, like a video editor timeline (think DaVinci Resolve). Any change between two versions has an `animation_progress: 0.0..1.0`. Drag the scrubber — all cards independently compute their appearance at that progress. Direction-independent: rewinding is literally reverse playback of the same animation, not a re-interpreted inverse diff. A deletion rewound is *the same red-fade animation running backward*, not an "addition" appearing green.

### Time-based timeline, not version-based
The history axis is real time, not commit count. Twenty commits in a refactoring hour cluster tightly; six months of untouched code stretch wide. This gives free semantic LOD: long-lived versions get visual prominence, bursts of activity self-cluster. Version-based axes hide this structure and don't scale to repo-wide history views.

### One state, derived rendering
There is one global state: `timeline_position: f32`, plus camera transform, plus selections. Every visual element computes itself as a pure function of that state. No event-driven animation orchestration. This is the architectural principle that makes scrubbing trivial and reverse-playback free.

## Visual language

### The world: three zones, three visual rules

The Ygg canvas has three distinct zones. Each has its own grammar. Mixing grammars is where "glow on glow" confusion comes from — don't.

**Zone 1 — The void.** The dark space outside any plate. Breathing near-black sky, slightly textured so it's not dead. *Rule*: objects in the void emit light — halos, bloom, outer glow. No shadows. Depth from brightness, sharpness, scale, blur. Think Tron, Mass Effect codex, Bourne Ultimatum surveillance screens.

**Zone 2 — The plate (scroll).** A floating luminous surface in the void, holding code content. *Rule*: the plate is a lit material — holographic parchment. It has two faces:
- **Outer edge** (facing the void): emits light outward as a soft bloom halo. This is the plate's presence in the void.
- **Top surface** (facing the viewer): a lit plane under implicit light from above. Objects laid on this surface are lit by it.

**Zone 3 — On the plate.** Cards, text, icons, handles — content placed on a lit surface. *Rule*: 2.5D physical objects with small drop shadows. Near-opaque tinted fills, subtle top-edge rim light from the implicit above-source. **No outer glow** — the plate lights them; they don't light themselves. Think objects on a glowing holographic table in sci-fi (Avengers, Minority Report): the *table* glows, the *objects on it* are physical and cast tiny shadows.

**Semantic lights: the exception.** Certain elements on the plate *are* allowed to emit — they're not decorative glow, they carry meaning. Specifically:
- Class armature / spine (genuinely luminous — it's a light-emitting rail built into the plate).
- Fold-state transitions (brief light pulses).
- Diff-change flashes (red/green/blue per operation type).
- Rolling edge during a fold animation.

These are functional lights, not ambient decoration. Keep the set small; new semantic lights require a real reason.

This grammar supersedes the older "everything emits light, no shadows anywhere" doctrine — that rule applied to *things in the void* and was misread as applying on the plate too. The unified rule now: **glow belongs to things floating in void or to semantic lights; shadows belong on lit surfaces.**

### Why not Material / paper-on-desk
Material Design / macOS paper-on-desk metaphors don't fit this canvas — they assume a page under ambient room light, no inherent luminosity. Ygg's plate *is* the light source. Shadows on the plate exist because the plate lights objects from below/around, not because there's a desk behind it.

### Cards
Methods and classes are rendered as **2.5D physical objects on the plate** ("cards") — not themselves luminous. Each card has:
- A structured header: keyword badge (`def`/`class`/`fn`/`pub`), name, signature with typed params as mini-columns, return type badge on the right. Keyword-only separators (`*`, `/`) visible as divider columns.
- A docstring sub-header in a distinct style, separated by a hairline.
- The body: code with syntax highlighting, grid-aligned monospace.
- A fold handle that reads as *honest UI affordance* — not a forgotten convention, but an inviting visual element.

Visual treatment (per Zone-3 rule):
- **Near-opaque tinted fill** — a "paper" or "plastic" material colored by semantics.
- **Tiny drop shadow** beneath the card (~2–3pt offset, low-alpha, softly blurred) — card sits *on* the plate.
- **Subtle top-edge rim light** — 1px bright line inside the top edge, cue for "lit from above."
- **No outer glow on the card itself.** Outer glow belongs to the plate (Zone 2) and to semantic lights.

Cards have a colored accent strip on the left encoding visibility/semantics:
- Public methods: bright accent, full opacity, elevated (a hair taller shadow).
- Private methods: muted accent, ~75% opacity, sitting lower (shorter shadow), slightly inset.
- Classmethod / staticmethod / associated functions: attached *directly* to the class armature, not the instance.
- Instance methods: connected via a lighter branch from a `self` node, not from the class armature directly.

Fold collapses a method into its header with a **rollup animation** — the body rolls up into the signature line, not a hard disappear.

### The class armature
A class has a strong visual "spine" — a vertical luminous rail running down its left side, rooted in a prominent class header with signature and docstring. Class/static methods attach rigidly to this spine (short, solid connector). Instance methods attach to a lighter `self` node that branches from the spine (softer, more organic connector). This is a teaching affordance: juniors can *see* that `classmethod` is structurally different from a regular method without having read PEP.

### Semantic-aware rendering
Same syntax, different meaning → different visuals. Key cases:
- Python `class C: a: int = 5` renders as class-variable (attached to armature). Add `@dataclass` → same syntax animates into instance-field style (attached to `self`).
- `@property` methods render as hybrid — signature like a method, but visually closer to an attribute.
- `@abstractmethod` / `...`-body methods render with a dashed/hollow card outline ("placeholder").
- Decorators are *visually attached* to their target card, not as a separate line above.

This requires per-language semantic rules beyond what tree-sitter provides. Start with Python; design the rule layer to be extensible.

### Diff animations
All diff visuals are parameterized by `progress: 0.0..1.0` and direction-independent.

- **Addition**: card materializes from nothing — slight expansion + green glow flash + fade-in to full opacity. At progress 0 it's absent; at progress 1 it's a normal card. Reversed scrubbing plays this backward: the card shrinks into a green flash into nothing.
- **Deletion**: card brightens to red, compresses, fades into darkness. Reversed: darkness → red flash → expands to normal card. The red-flash identity is preserved in both directions — you always recognize "this was a deletion" regardless of scrub direction.
- **Modification**: intra-card changes — individual lines animate (green/red flashes for added/removed sub-lines).
- **Move** (same content, different position): blue-tinted glow, card slides to new position, no red/green. Recognizable as "this didn't change, it relocated."
- **Move + modify**: composed animation — position interpolates and inner content animates simultaneously.
- **Rename** (file or symbol): letters morph between old and new name; the node in the file tree smoothly relocates/rewrites. If combined with move or modify, compose as above.
- **Copy/fork** (file copied with `git diff -C` detection): not "appeared from nothing" but a cell-division animation from the ancestor node. Optional dotted ancestry link persists.

### Panes as plates, not boxes
UI panes (file tree on left, code plate on right) are not walled-off boxes with borders — they're **plates** (Zone 2): luminous surfaces floating in the void. The plate's outer bloom into the void doubles as the "pane is here" cue; no borders or splitters needed. Hover or focus-into-a-plate → the plate's bloom brightens slightly ("scroll here, interactions target this region"). All over a unified void.

The void has a subtle, slow ambient animation (breathing sky + faint drifting clouds at very low contrast) to feel alive without distracting.

### Overlays, not sidebars
Inspired by game engine editors (Unity, Blender) more than by code editors. Timeline scrubber, navigation controls, legends float as semi-transparent HUD overlays *over* the main canvas, not as fixed bottom/side panels eating into it. Draggable, dismissable.

### 3D vs 2.5D — hybrid by zone
Each zone has its own rule:

- **Zone 1 (void)**: objects float in pseudo-3D space, orthographic + frontal projection by default. Depth cues in the void come from glow, opacity, scale, blur — not geometry. Camera movements (zoom, fly-through) feel 3D without actually rotating individual plates. Plates *can* rotate here (future: repo-browse history pages, branch-graph tilts) because they're 3D quads in the pipeline from M3.1 onward.
- **Zone 3 (on the plate)**: 2.5D — flat-with-shadows. Physical objects lying on the plate's top surface. Shadows cue depth. No rotation of items within a plate; they stay parallel to the plate's own surface.

This hybrid is intentional: the void gets holographic FUI flow; the plate surface gets paper-on-a-lit-desk legibility. The two aesthetics sit on different sides of the plate's edge and don't fight each other.

### Git branch graphs (future)
Branches that diverge and converge are inherently a graph structure. A future exploration is tilting the branch graph slightly toward 3D to make merge topology legible. Not in prototype scope — noted for later.

## Timeline widget

The timeline is the single most distinctive UI element. Inspired by video editor timelines (DaVinci Resolve, Premiere), not by git log listings.

### Time-based axis
Position on the timeline = real calendar time of the commit. Clusters of activity appear as dense regions; quiet periods stretch wide. This is the canvas on which everything else is drawn.

### Significance as a second dimension
Commits have a *weight* computed from:
- Delta size (lines, files, methods touched)
- Structural vs. cosmetic changes (new class > renamed variable)
- API surface impact (public vs. internal changes)
- Surrounding density (an isolated commit among silence outweighs one of 50 in an hour)
- Duration of subsequent quiet (if a file wasn't touched for 6 months after commit X, X was probably important)
- Explicit markers: tags, release branches, merge commits of PRs — these are the railroad spikes of the timeline, always visible at any zoom.

Weight maps to visual prominence: bar height, brightness, label visibility. A cosmetic commit and a major refactor that are temporally adjacent render *very differently*.

Weight is computed once during an indexing phase when a repo is first opened, then cached — the git equivalent of `git gc`/`repack`: expensive prep, cheap access. Re-indexing is incremental as new commits arrive.

### Nested zoom brackets
Instead of fumbling with hover-detection of invisible clusters, the timeline draws **visible nested brackets** around the current zoom focus and its sub-clusters.

Mental model:
- The outer bracket frames the currently-focused time range (your "zoom-full" zone).
- Above the outer bracket, a visible affordance lets you zoom *out* to the parent range.
- Inside the outer bracket, one or more child brackets highlight sub-clusters you could zoom *into*.
- Click a child bracket → smooth zoom into it. That becomes the new outer, and *its* children appear. Click outside all brackets but within the current frame → nothing, you're already there. Click the outer's top affordance → zoom out one level.

Bracket colors are muted for "you are here" (outer) and brighter for "come explore" (inner children). As you descend, the former inner becomes the new outer and dims.

This works for mouse *and* touch: no hover detection required, brackets are persistent visual objects. On touch, pinch-zoom still works for freeform zoom; brackets provide smart-snap to semantic boundaries. Tap targets are naturally large.

### LOD as a function of zoom
Like Google Maps showing region labels only at certain zoom levels, timeline elements appear/disappear based on scale:
- Most zoomed out: only major epochs / tagged releases / big refactors.
- Mid zoom: significant commits.
- Fully zoomed in: every commit.
This uses the weight metric. Number of visible items per zoom level stays in the 5–9 range — readable, not overwhelming.

### Previews on the timeline
At sufficient zoom, individual commits (or clusters) render thumbnail previews of the file/directory state — tiny card silhouettes, miniature class shapes. Like video editor clip thumbnails. This makes skimming history feel like skimming a storyboard.

### Scope
Initially: per-file timeline (the file open in the code pane has its own timeline above or below). Future: project-wide timeline showing the whole repo's life, with its own navigation idioms. The same widget architecture should extend to both.

## Architecture

### Three components

**1. Analyzer** — reads git repo + source files, computes semantic diff between arbitrary commits, produces a structured `SceneDescription` (the input to the renderer). Owns: tree-sitter parsing, git access, rename/copy detection, move-method detection via AST matching (GumTree-style algorithm), weight computation, indexing/caching.

**2. Renderer** — consumes a `SceneDescription` + a `ViewState` (timeline_position, camera, selection) and draws the scene. Owns: card layout, animations, glow effects, text rendering, scroll virtualization, input handling.

**3. CLI entry points** — wire modes to components.

### Modes (stable from day one)

CLI shape, locked in from Milestone 1 to avoid monolith-refactoring later:

```
ygg <file>                     show one file
ygg <dir>                      show a tree + code
ygg <dir> --git                add git history / timeline
ygg diff <ref1> <ref2>         diff mode between two refs
ygg diff <ref1> <ref2> <path>  diff a specific file/subtree
```

Modes differ in what data the analyzer prepares and which UI regions the renderer shows. The core scene-description format is unified across modes.

### Stack

- **Language**: Rust throughout.
- **GPU**: `wgpu` for the main canvas (cards, spatial layout, animations).
- **Text rendering**: `glyphon` (or a small custom monospace atlas renderer on top of wgpu — monospace is trivial; evaluate which is less friction).
- **Syntax**: `tree-sitter` with language-specific grammars, starting with Python. A thin semantic-rules layer sits above tree-sitter to recognize decorators like `@dataclass`, `@classmethod`, `@property`, etc.
- **Overlays / HUD**: `egui` (timeline scrubber, file tree pane, controls).
- **Windowing**: `winit`.
- **Git**: `gix` (gitoxide) preferred; fall back to `git2`/libgit2 if something critical is missing.
- **Diff algorithm**: custom AST-matching (GumTree-style) over tree-sitter ASTs. Git's `-M`/`-C` for file-level rename/copy detection.

### Why not a web-based stack
Considered and rejected. Electron/Webview apps chew 300MB to open a 10-line note. Browsers excel at existing web *content*; they're overkill and sluggish as a rendering host for a specialized spatial viewer. Since Ygg is a *viewer* (no text editing, no IME, no selection-with-drag complexity), the traditionally hard "build a text editor" parts don't apply — monospace text on GPU is a straightforward atlas-of-glyphs + quads problem. We keep the app native, small, fast.

### Why not GPUI (Zed's framework)
Considered. GPUI is excellent for traditional-layout editors with panes/splits/borders. Ygg wants floating overlays, a luminous void, non-pane UI organization — more like a game engine editor. Fighting GPUI's layout model is more cost than benefit. wgpu + egui gives us exactly the mental model we want: spatial canvas underneath, immediate-mode floating overlays on top.

### State model

One central `AppState`:

```rust
struct AppState {
    scene: SceneDescription,        // what to draw (from analyzer)
    view: ViewState,                // camera, selection, focus
    timeline_position: f32,         // 0.0..1.0 within current range
    timeline_range: TimeRange,      // absolute time window currently framed
    ui_overlays: OverlayState,      // egui panel positions, visibility
}
```

Every render frame: scene + view + timeline_position → per-card computed visual state (position, scale, opacity, glow, animation progress). Pure derivation. No events-mutate-animations dance.

### Indexing and caching

When a repo is opened: analyzer walks history, parses each relevant file version, computes weights, detects moves/renames/copies, builds per-file timelines, and persists an index next to the repo (e.g., `.ygg/index/...`). Subsequent opens are instant; new commits trigger incremental updates.

## Language expansion

Prototype targets **Python** only. The semantic-rules layer is designed for extension:
- Each supported language has a module with: tree-sitter grammar binding, token→color mapping, a set of semantic rules (decorator handling, visibility conventions, class/instance distinction markers).
- Rust, TypeScript, Java, Go are natural next languages — each exposes "this belongs to the type, not the instance" in its own syntax but the visual representation (attachment to armature vs. `self` node) is *shared across languages*. This is part of the appeal: Ygg's visual language transcends syntactic surface.

## Testing philosophy

Use `rstest` for parameterized tests. One parameterized test per logical function covering all its cases, not ten single-case tests.

**Test the logic, don't test the rendering.**

- Covered: semantic diff, git access, AST matching, weight computation, timeline interpolation, layout calculations, rename/copy detection.
- Not covered by automated tests: GPU rendering output, visual effects, animation smoothness. Snapshot-testing a wgpu canvas costs more than the value it provides at this stage — verify rendering by eye.

Tests should maximize coverage per line of test code, not per assertion. If two cases fit in one parameterized test, that's one test.

## What this is not

- Not an editor. No text input. (If this ever changes, it's a separate project.)
- Not a replacement for VS Code or Zed. Complements them.
- Not trying to "fix git" — git is fine. Ygg reads from git, doesn't replace it.
- Not a general diff tool for arbitrary files. Semantic awareness is source-code-specific.
- Not a collaboration/realtime tool. Single-user, local-first.

## Tone for AI-assisted development

When Claude Code (or similar) works on this codebase:
- Architectural principles above are load-bearing; don't bypass them for local convenience.
- Prefer adding to the semantic-rules layer over hard-coding language specifics into the renderer.
- When in doubt about visual design, re-read the **Visual language** section; the luminous-void aesthetic is not negotiable in the current prototype.
- When in doubt about state, re-read the **One state, derived rendering** principle. Avoid event-driven animation code.
- Write the smallest parameterized test that covers a logical unit. Don't pad tests for coverage metrics.

Milestones and concrete tasks live in `TASKS.md`.
